questions.question_text: Under Maryland law, when is dual agency permitted?
questions.correct_answer: When both parties give informed written consent
distractors: When the broker verbally informs both parties
Only in commercial transactions
When the listing agent obtains seller approval
questions.explanation: Maryland permits dual agency only with the informed written consent of both parties, documented on the Consent for Dual Agency form (BOP §17-530.1). The dual agent is the broker or a designated branch office manager — not the individual agents — and a party who signed earlier must affirm consent for the specific property and buyer before an offer is presented or accepted.

questions.question_text: A Maryland agent has been working with a buyer for weeks sharing pricing strategy. The buyer now wants to offer on the agent's own listing, and both buyer and seller want to keep their own representation. How must the brokerage proceed?
questions.correct_answer: One agent cannot serve both sides; the broker must designate another agent for one party
distractors: The agent may represent both parties once written consent is obtained
The broker simply notifies the Maryland Real Estate Commission
The agent verbally discloses the dual role at the next showing
questions.explanation: Under BOP §17-530.1, the dual agent is the broker (or designated branch office manager), who must assign one intra-company agent for the seller and a DIFFERENT one for the buyer — a single licensee may never represent both sides, even with consent. With both parties' written consent to dual agency, the broker designates another affiliated agent for one party; if either party refuses, the brokerage relationship must be restructured or ended for that transaction.

questions.question_text: A dual agent in Maryland learns the buyer will pay $20,000 above asking price. What should the agent do with this information?
questions.correct_answer: Keep it confidential unless the buyer consents
distractors: Share it with the seller as a material fact
Disclose it to both parties equally
Report it to the supervising broker only
questions.explanation: A Maryland dual agent — the broker or designated branch office manager — may not disclose either party's confidential information without that party's written consent (BOP §17-530.1). A buyer's willingness to pay above asking is expressly protected; it may not be shared with the seller unless the buyer authorizes it in writing.

questions.question_text: In Maryland intra-company agency, which party does the brokerage itself represent?
questions.correct_answer: Both the buyer and the seller, without undivided loyalty to either
distractors: Neither the buyer nor the seller
The party who listed the property first
The seller, as the original client
questions.explanation: Maryland's intra-company agents operate inside a dual agency: the broker (or designated branch office manager) is the dual agent for BOTH the seller and the buyer (BOP §17-530.1), while each intra-company agent exclusively serves their own client. As dual agent, the broker cannot act exclusively for either party and owes undivided loyalty to neither.

questions.question_text: Agent Kim and Agent Lee work for the same Maryland brokerage. Kim represents the seller and Lee represents the buyer in a transaction. Kim discovers the buyer plans to resell the property quickly for profit. What is Kim's obligation?
questions.correct_answer: Kim owes no duty to the buyer and keeps seller focus
distractors: Kim must share this with the seller immediately
Kim must disclose it to the brokerage manager
Kim should advise Lee to withdraw the offer
questions.explanation: Kim, the seller's intra-company agent, owes fiduciary duties only to the seller and none to the buyer. Note that in a Maryland dual agency, dual agents and intra-company agents may not disclose either party's confidential information — including motivations like a plan to resell — to the other side without written consent; only material facts about the property itself must be disclosed to all parties.

questions.question_text: Which statement BEST describes a dual agent's role in Maryland?
questions.correct_answer: The agent must remain impartial to both parties
distractors: The agent advocates primarily for the seller
The agent may favor the party who pays commission
The agent has no fiduciary duties to either party
questions.explanation: A Maryland dual agent — the broker or the broker's designated branch office manager — does not exclusively represent either party and owes undivided loyalty to neither (BOP §17-530.1). The dual agent must treat both sides impartially and may not disclose one party's confidential information to the other.

questions.question_text: A Maryland buyer asks to see a property listed by another agent in the same brokerage. The buyer's agent explains intra-company agency. What consent is required?
questions.correct_answer: Written informed consent from both the buyer and the seller
distractors: Only the buyer needs to provide consent
The supervising broker's approval is sufficient
No consent is needed if agents are separate
questions.explanation: Intra-company agency can occur only within a consented dual agency: BOP §17-530.1 requires the WRITTEN informed consent of all parties (the Consent for Dual Agency form) before the broker may act as dual agent and designate intra-company agents for each side. Consent must also be affirmed for the specific property and buyer before an offer is presented or accepted.

questions.question_text: Within a Maryland dual agency, how does an intra-company agent's role differ from the dual agent's role?
questions.correct_answer: Each intra-company agent owes full fiduciary duties to their own client
distractors: Intra-company agents must remain neutral between the parties
Intra-company agents need no written consent from the parties
Only the seller's intra-company agent retains fiduciary duties
questions.explanation: Intra-company agency is not an alternative to dual agency in Maryland — it is how dual agency operates. The broker (dual agent) acts for both parties and owes undivided loyalty to neither, while each designated intra-company agent gives their own client the same full services as an ordinary agent, including advice on price and negotiation strategy (BOP §17-530.1).

questions.question_text: A Maryland seller tells a dual agent about a major plumbing defect but asks the agent not to tell the buyer. How should the agent respond?
questions.correct_answer: Explain that latent defects must still be disclosed
distractors: Honor the seller's request for confidentiality
Withdraw from the dual agency immediately
Disclose it only if the buyer asks directly
questions.explanation: While dual agents must keep confidential information private, latent defects that pose a threat to health or safety must always be disclosed regardless of agency relationship. The agent should explain this legal obligation to the seller.

questions.question_text: In Maryland intra-company agency, what fiduciary duties does a designated buyer's agent owe?
questions.correct_answer: Full fiduciary duties to the buyer only
distractors: Limited duties shared between both parties
Fiduciary duties to the brokerage first
Advisory duties but not full fiduciary duties
questions.explanation: Under Maryland's intra-company agency framework, each designated agent owes full fiduciary duties exclusively to their own client. The designated buyer's agent represents only the buyer with complete loyalty, confidentiality, and advocacy.
